description:
You are an IT-support technician who has just received a ticket complaining that a recent source-tree backup left behind many “*.bak” files inside the main project directory.  
Your goal is to remove every “*.bak” file that lives anywhere under  

    /home/user/project  

and to keep a permanent record of exactly which files were removed.

REQUIREMENTS the automated grader will check:

1. All regular files whose names end with “.bak” (case-sensitive) that are located anywhere beneath “/home/user/project” must be deleted.  
   • No other files may be touched.  
   • The directory layout itself must remain intact.

2. You must accomplish the deletion with **one single terminal command** that combines “find” and “xargs” (pipes are allowed).  
   • Do not use “while read”, “for”, or external scripting languages.  
   • Any options you need may be supplied to either find or xargs.

3. While deleting, you must simultaneously record the absolute path of every file that gets deleted into  

    /home/user/cleanup_log.txt  

   The command must create that file automatically.  
   • Each deleted file path must appear on its own line.  
   • The lines must be in strictly ascending ASCII (alphabetical) order.  
   • No blank lines, no extra spaces, no headers or footers—just the paths.

4. After your command finishes:  
   • “/home/user/cleanup_log.txt” must exist and be world-readable (e.g., 0644).  
   • No “*.bak” files may remain anywhere under “/home/user/project”.

HINTS / ALLOWED TOOLS  
• “find” supports “-name”, “-type”, “-print”, “-delete”, etc.  
• You may pipe the list through “sort” before feeding it to xargs so the log is alphabetically ordered.  
• Remember that the same list you feed to xargs must also be what you write to the log—`tee` can help.

Do the work in a live shell session; do not just describe the steps. The grader will open a fresh container where the directory tree (with several “*.bak” files) is already present and will verify:

A. “/home/user/cleanup_log.txt” exists with exactly four non-empty lines, alphabetically sorted.  
B. Each line points to a file that no longer exists.  
C. No other files were removed or altered.

Once you believe the ticket is resolved, you’re done—no extra output is required beyond what your command prints to the terminal.
truth:
Initial filesystem before the agent starts:

/home/user/project/
/home/user/project/src/
/home/user/project/src/main.c        (regular file, keep)
/home/user/project/src/main.c.bak    (regular file, delete)
/home/user/project/src/util.c        (regular file, keep)
/home/user/project/src/util.c.bak    (regular file, delete)
/home/user/project/docs/
/home/user/project/docs/readme.md    (regular file, keep)
/home/user/project/docs/readme.md.bak (regular file, delete)
/home/user/project/tmp/
/home/user/project/tmp/notes.txt     (regular file, keep)
/home/user/project/tmp/notes.txt.bak (regular file, delete)
/home/user/project/tmp/old.tmp       (regular file, keep)

No “/home/user/cleanup_log.txt” is present initially.

Expected final state AFTER the agent runs the correct single “find | sort | tee | xargs” style command:

1. The four “*.bak” files listed above are gone.  
2. All non-“.bak” files remain untouched.  
3. /home/user/cleanup_log.txt exists, mode 0644, containing exactly:

/home/user/project/docs/readme.md.bak
/home/user/project/src/main.c.bak
/home/user/project/src/util.c.bak
/home/user/project/tmp/notes.txt.bak

(Each line terminated with a single newline, no carriage returns.)  
No extra characters, spaces, or lines.

Automated tests will confirm:
• `! [ -e <each bak path> ]`  
• `cat /home/user/cleanup_log.txt` matches the four-line string above byte-for-byte.  
• `find /home/user/project -name '*.bak'` produces no output.
